content.question: What is semantic HTML? Why does it matter for accessibility and SEO?
content.answer:
Semantic HTML uses elements that describe their meaning (header, nav, main, article, section, footer) instead of generic divs. This helps:

1. Accessibility: Screen readers understand the page structure
2. SEO: Search engines understand content hierarchy and importance
3. Maintainability: Code is self-documenting
4. Browser features: Reader mode, outline view work correctly

codeExamples.title: Semantic vs Non-Semantic
codeExamples.code:
Home
Blog Post
Content here...
Copyright 2024
Home

Blog Post

Content here...

Copyright 2024
codeExamples.explanation: Screen readers announce: 'navigation landmark', 'main content', 'article'. With divs, users hear nothing useful about the page structure.

codeExamples.title: ARIA Attributes When Needed
codeExamples.code:
X
3 items in cart
We'll never share your email
codeExamples.explanation: Rule: use native HTML elements first (they have built-in accessibility). Only add ARIA when native elements can't express the semantics you need.

codeExamples.title: Common Accessibility Checklist
codeExamples.code:
Email is required

Page Title

Section

Subsection

Another Section

Skip to main content
codeExamples.explanation: Accessibility isn't just about screen readers - it helps keyboard users, people with low vision, cognitive disabilities, and everyone on mobile.

notes: ARIA first rule: don't use ARIA if a native HTML element can do the job
Test with keyboard-only navigation (Tab, Enter, Escape, Arrow keys)
Use Lighthouse in Chrome DevTools for accessibility auditing
followUps: What is the difference between aria-label and aria-labelledby?
How do you make a custom dropdown accessible?
What are WCAG guidelines and compliance levels (A, AA, AAA)?
interviewTips: Mentioning accessibility in any frontend interview is a strong signal
Know at least 5 semantic elements and when to use each
Show you've actually tested with screen readers or keyboard navigation

content.question: Explain the CSS box model. What is the difference between content-box and border-box?
content.answer:
Every element is a rectangular box with:
- Content: the actual content area
- Padding: space between content and border
- Border: the border around padding
- Margin: space outside the border

box-sizing: content-box (default) - width/height only applies to content. Padding and border are added on top.
box-sizing: border-box - width/height includes content + padding + border. Much more predictable.

Margin collapsing: adjacent vertical margins collapse into the larger value, not additive.

codeExamples.title: content-box vs border-box
codeExamples.explanation: border-box makes sizing predictable. Always apply the global reset so every element uses border-box.

codeExamples.title: Margin Collapsing
codeExamples.explanation: Margin collapsing only happens vertically between block elements. Flex and grid containers prevent it entirely.

codeExamples.title: Visualizing the Box Model
codeExamples.code: Content
codeExamples.explanation: Use Chrome DevTools Computed tab to visualize the box model of any element. Hover over the diagram to highlight each layer on the page.

notes: Always set *, *::before, *::after { box-sizing: border-box } globally
Margin collapsing is a common source of unexpected spacing bugs
Use padding for internal spacing, margin for external spacing between elements
followUps: When do margins NOT collapse?
What is the difference between padding and margin?
How does overflow affect the box model?
interviewTips: Draw the box model diagram - interviewers expect it
Always mention the border-box reset as a best practice
Know the margin collapsing rules - it trips up many candidates

content.question: How do you implement responsive design? What is mobile-first approach?
content.answer:
Responsive design adapts layouts to different screen sizes. Mobile-first means:

1. Base styles target mobile (smallest screens)
2. Use min-width media queries to add complexity for larger screens
3. Progressive enhancement: start simple, add features

Key techniques:
- Fluid layouts with %, vw, fr units
- clamp() for fluid typography
- Media queries at breakpoints
- Responsive images with srcset/picture
- Container queries for component-level responsiveness

codeExamples.title: Mobile-First Responsive Layout
codeExamples.explanation: Mobile-first uses min-width queries to progressively enhance. Desktop-first uses max-width to remove features. Mobile-first is the industry standard.

codeExamples.title: Responsive Images
codeExamples.explanation: srcset lets the browser choose the optimal resolution. picture element gives you full art-direction control for different crops at different sizes.

codeExamples.title: Viewport Meta Tag
codeExamples.code:
<!-- Required in every responsive page -->
<meta name="viewport"
      content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1" />

<!-- Without this, mobile browsers render at
     ~980px width and scale down, making
     media queries useless -->

<style>
/* Common responsive utilities */
.visually-hidden {
  position: absolute;
  width: 1px;
  height: 1px;
  overflow: hidden;
  clip: rect(0 0 0 0);
}

/* Hide on mobile, show on desktop */
.desktop-only {
  display: none;
}
@media (min-width: 1024px) {
  .desktop-only { display: block; }
}
</style>
codeExamples.explanation: The viewport meta tag is essential. Without it, mobile browsers assume a 980px viewport and media queries won't trigger correctly.

notes: Always include the viewport meta tag in your HTML head
Use clamp() for fluid typography instead of multiple media queries
Test on real devices, not just browser DevTools resize
followUps: What is the difference between srcset and picture?
How do container queries differ from media queries?
What breakpoints do you typically use?
interviewTips: Know the mobile-first approach and explain WHY (progressive enhancement)
Mention clamp() for fluid typography - shows modern CSS knowledge
Be ready to code a responsive layout from scratch

content.question: Explain the structure of an HTML document. What goes in the head vs body?
content.answer:
An HTML document has a strict structure:

1. <!DOCTYPE html> - tells browser to use standards mode
2. <html lang="en"> - root element with language
3. <head> - metadata, not visible: title, meta, links, scripts
4. <body> - visible content

The head contains:
- <title> for browser tab/SEO
- <meta> for charset, viewport, description
- <link> for stylesheets, favicons
- <script> for JS (prefer defer/async or at body end)

The body contains all visible page content.

codeExamples.title: Complete HTML5 Boilerplate
codeExamples.code:
Page Heading

Content goes here.
codeExamples.explanation: This is the minimal production-ready HTML5 boilerplate. Every element has a purpose.

codeExamples.title: Script Loading Strategies
codeExamples.code:
<!-- 1. Default: blocks parsing -->
<script src="app.js"></script>
<!-- Parser stops, downloads, executes, then continues -->

<!-- 2. async: downloads parallel, executes ASAP -->
<script async src="analytics.js"></script>
<!-- Good for independent scripts (analytics, ads) -->

<!-- 3. defer: downloads parallel, executes after parse -->
<script defer src="app.js"></script>
<!-- Best for app scripts that need the DOM -->

<!-- 4. type=module: deferred by default -->
<script type="module" src="app.mjs"></script>
<!-- ES modules are always deferred -->

<!-- Loading order with multiple scripts:
  defer: preserves order (a.js then b.js)
  async: no guaranteed order -->
codeExamples.explanation: Use defer for your main app bundle. Use async for third-party scripts that don't depend on your code. Never block rendering with synchronous scripts in the head.

codeExamples.title: DOCTYPE and Quirks Mode
codeExamples.code: Bonjour le monde
codeExamples.explanation: DOCTYPE html is the shortest valid doctype. It triggers standards mode in all browsers. Without it, browsers use quirks mode with unpredictable CSS behavior.

notes: DOCTYPE must be the very first thing in the document, before <html>
charset meta tag should be within the first 1024 bytes of the document
Use defer for scripts, not async, when execution order matters
followUps: What is the difference between async and defer?
What happens if you omit the DOCTYPE?
Why is the lang attribute important?
interviewTips: Being able to write a boilerplate from memory shows solid fundamentals
Know the difference between async and defer - very common question
Mention preconnect/preload for performance bonus points

content.question: How do HTML forms work? What input types are available and how do you validate them?
content.answer:
HTML forms collect user input and submit it to a server. Key concepts:

1. <form> wraps all inputs with action (URL) and method (GET/POST)
2. Input types: text, email, password, number, date, file, checkbox, radio, range, color, tel, url, search
3. Built-in validation: required, pattern, min/max, minlength/maxlength
4. Labels are required for accessibility
5. FormData API for JavaScript form handling

HTML5 added many input types that provide native validation and mobile-optimized keyboards.

codeExamples.title: Form with Various Input Types
codeExamples.code:
<form action="/api/register" method="POST">
  <!-- Text with pattern validation -->
  <label for="username">Username</label>
  <input id="username" type="text" name="username"
         required minlength="3" maxlength="20"
         pattern="[a-zA-Z0-9_]+"
         placeholder="john_doe" />

  <!-- Email (validates format) -->
  <label for="email">Email</label>
  <input id="email" type="email" name="email"
         required />

  <!-- Password -->
  <label for="password">Password</label>
  <input id="password" type="password" name="password"
         required minlength="8" />

  <!-- Number with range -->
  <label for="age">Age</label>
  <input id="age" type="number" name="age"
         min="18" max="120" />

  <!-- Date picker -->
  <label for="dob">Date of Birth</label>
  <input id="dob" type="date" name="dob" />

  <button type="submit">Register</button>
</form>
codeExamples.explanation: HTML5 input types give you free validation and mobile keyboards. type=email shows @ keyboard, type=number shows numeric pad, type=date shows date picker.

codeExamples.title: Radio, Checkbox, Select
codeExamples.code:
<form>
  <!-- Radio: only one selection -->
  <fieldset>
    <legend>Plan</legend>
    <label>
      <input type="radio" name="plan" value="free"
             checked />
      Free
    </label>
    <label>
      <input type="radio" name="plan" value="pro" />
      Pro
    </label>
  </fieldset>

  <!-- Checkbox: multiple selections -->
  <fieldset>
    <legend>Skills</legend>
    <label>
      <input type="checkbox" name="skills"
             value="html" />
      HTML
    </label>
    <label>
      <input type="checkbox" name="skills"
             value="css" />
      CSS
    </label>
  </fieldset>

  <!-- Select dropdown -->
  <label for="country">Country</label>
  <select id="country" name="country">
    <option value="">Select...</option>
    <option value="us">United States</option>
    <option value="in">India</option>
  </select>
</form>
codeExamples.explanation: Use fieldset/legend to group related inputs. Radio buttons with the same name are mutually exclusive. Checkboxes allow multiple selections.

codeExamples.title: FormData API in JavaScript
codeExamples.code:
<form id="myForm">
  <input name="title" value="Hello" />
  <input type="file" name="avatar" />
  <button type="submit">Submit</button>
</form>

<script>
const form = document.getElementById('myForm');
form.addEventListener('submit', async (e) => {
  e.preventDefault();

  const formData = new FormData(form);
  // formData.get('title') => 'Hello'
  // formData.get('avatar') => File object

  // Add extra data
  formData.append('timestamp', Date.now());

  // Send with fetch
  const res = await fetch('/api/upload', {
    method: 'POST',
    body: formData,
    // Do NOT set Content-Type header
    // Browser sets it with boundary automatically
  });
});
</script>
codeExamples.explanation: FormData handles file uploads and multipart data. Never manually set Content-Type when using FormData - the browser adds the correct multipart boundary.

notes: Always use label elements - either wrapping the input or using for/id
Use fieldset and legend for grouping related radio/checkbox inputs
HTML validation is for UX only - always validate on the server too
followUps: How do you handle file uploads in HTML forms?
What is the difference between GET and POST form methods?
How do you create a custom-styled file input?
interviewTips: Know at least 8-10 input types from memory
Always mention server-side validation alongside client-side
Show you understand form accessibility (labels, fieldsets)

content.question: When should you use tables vs other layout methods? What are the different list types?
content.answer:
Tables are ONLY for tabular data (rows and columns of related data). Never use tables for page layout.

Table structure: table > thead/tbody/tfoot > tr > th/td
- th for headers, td for data cells
- caption for table title
- scope attribute on th for accessibility

List types:
- <ul> unordered list (bullets)
- <ol> ordered list (numbers)
- <dl> description list (term/definition pairs)
- Lists can be nested

codeExamples.title: Accessible Data Table
codeExamples.code:
Q4 Sales Report
Product Units Revenue
Widget A 1,200 $24,000
Widget B 850 $17,000
Total 2,050 $41,000
codeExamples.explanation: scope='col' and scope='row' help screen readers associate data cells with their headers. caption provides a title that screen readers announce.

codeExamples.title: List Types
codeExamples.code:
• HTML
  • CSS
  • JavaScript
  1. Third item (renders as c.)
  2. Fourth item (renders as d.)
HTML
HyperText Markup Language
CSS
Cascading Style Sheets
Used for styling web pages
  • Frontend
    • React
    • Vue
  • Backend
codeExamples.explanation: Use ul for unordered items, ol when order matters, dl for key-value pairs like glossaries or metadata.

codeExamples.title: Styling Tables with CSS
codeExamples.explanation: border-collapse: collapse is essential for clean table borders. Wrap tables in a scrollable container for mobile responsiveness.

notes: Never use tables for layout - only for actual tabular data
Use border-collapse: collapse for clean table styling
Description lists (dl/dt/dd) are underused but great for metadata
followUps: How do you make a responsive table on mobile?
What is the difference between border-collapse and border-spacing?
How do screen readers navigate tables?
interviewTips: Emphasize that tables are for data, not layout
Know the complete table structure: caption, thead, tbody, tfoot
Mention scope attribute for accessibility

content.question: What are the key HTML5 APIs? Explain localStorage vs sessionStorage.
content.answer:
HTML5 introduced powerful browser APIs:

1. Web Storage: localStorage (persists) vs sessionStorage (per tab)
2. Geolocation: get user's location with permission
3. Web Workers: background threads for heavy computation
4. History API: manipulate browser history (SPA routing)
5. Canvas/WebGL: 2D/3D graphics
6. Drag and Drop: native drag/drop support

localStorage vs sessionStorage:
- localStorage: persists until manually cleared, shared across tabs
- sessionStorage: cleared when tab closes, per-tab isolation
- Both: ~5MB limit, synchronous, strings only

codeExamples.title: localStorage vs sessionStorage
codeExamples.explanation: localStorage persists forever (until cleared). sessionStorage is isolated per tab. Both store strings only - use JSON.stringify/parse for objects.

codeExamples.title: Geolocation API
codeExamples.explanation: Geolocation requires user permission (HTTPS only in production). Always handle errors gracefully - the user may deny access.

codeExamples.title: History API for SPA Routing
codeExamples.explanation: React Router, Vue Router, and other SPA routers are built on top of the History API. pushState changes the URL without a page reload.

notes: localStorage is synchronous and blocks the main thread - don't store large data
Geolocation only works on HTTPS in production
Web Workers can't access the DOM - communicate via postMessage
followUps: What are cookies vs localStorage vs sessionStorage?
How do Web Workers communicate with the main thread?
What is IndexedDB and when would you use it over localStorage?
interviewTips: Know the storage limits: cookies ~4KB, localStorage ~5MB, IndexedDB ~unlimited
Mention security: localStorage is vulnerable to XSS attacks
Show awareness that these APIs require feature detection

content.question: What are meta tags? How do they affect SEO and social sharing?
content.answer:
Meta tags provide metadata about the HTML document. They don't display on the page but are read by browsers, search engines, and social platforms.

Key meta tags:
1. charset - character encoding (always UTF-8)
2. viewport - mobile rendering control
3. description - search result snippet (150-160 chars)
4. robots - control indexing/following
5. Open Graph (og:) - social media previews
6. Twitter Card - Twitter-specific previews

SEO best practices: unique title/description per page, proper heading hierarchy, semantic HTML, fast loading.

codeExamples.title: Essential Meta Tags
codeExamples.code:
<head>
  <!-- Character encoding -->
  <meta charset="UTF-8" />

  <!-- Viewport for mobile -->
  <meta name="viewport"
        content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1" />

  <!-- SEO -->
  <title>Best Coding Bootcamps 2025 | LearnCode</title>
  <meta name="description"
        content="Compare top coding bootcamps.
        Find the best program for your career goals." />

  <!-- Robots -->
  <meta name="robots" content="index, follow" />
  <!-- noindex: don't show in search results -->
  <!-- nofollow: don't follow links on this page -->

  <!-- Canonical URL (avoid duplicate content) -->
  <link rel="canonical"
        href="https://example.com/bootcamps" />

  <!-- Language alternatives -->
  <link rel="alternate" hreflang="es"
        href="https://example.com/es/bootcamps" />
</head>
codeExamples.explanation: Every page needs charset, viewport, title, and description. Canonical URLs prevent duplicate content penalties when the same content has multiple URLs.

codeExamples.title: Open Graph & Twitter Cards
codeExamples.code:
<!-- Open Graph (Facebook, LinkedIn, etc.) -->
<meta property="og:title"
      content="Best Coding Bootcamps 2025" />
<meta property="og:description"
      content="Compare top coding bootcamps." />
<meta property="og:image"
      content="https://example.com/og-image.jpg" />
<meta property="og:url"
      content="https://example.com/bootcamps" />
<meta property="og:type" content="article" />
<meta property="og:site_name" content="LearnCode" />

<!-- Twitter Card -->
<meta name="twitter:card"
      content="summary_large_image" />
<meta name="twitter:title"
      content="Best Coding Bootcamps 2025" />
<meta name="twitter:description"
      content="Compare top coding bootcamps." />
<meta name="twitter:image"
      content="https://example.com/twitter-card.jpg" />

<!-- OG image should be 1200x630px
     Twitter image should be 1200x600px -->
codeExamples.explanation: Open Graph controls how your page appears when shared on social media. Without these tags, platforms guess from page content, often with poor results.

codeExamples.title: Structured Data (JSON-LD)
codeExamples.code:
<!-- JSON-LD structured data for rich snippets -->
<script type="application/ld+json">
{
  "@context": "https://schema.org",
  "@type": "Article",
  "headline": "Best Coding Bootcamps 2025",
  "author": {
    "@type": "Person",
    "name": "Jane Smith"
  },
  "datePublished": "2025-01-15",
  "image": "https://example.com/article-image.jpg",
  "publisher": {
    "@type": "Organization",
    "name": "LearnCode",
    "logo": {
      "@type": "ImageObject",
      "url": "https://example.com/logo.png"
    }
  }
}
</script>

<!-- Other types: Product, FAQ, Event,
     BreadcrumbList, JobPosting, etc. -->
codeExamples.explanation: Structured data helps Google show rich snippets (stars, prices, FAQs) in search results. JSON-LD is the recommended format by Google.

notes: Title should be 50-60 characters, description 150-160 characters
Each page should have a unique title and description
Test Open Graph tags with Facebook Sharing Debugger or Twitter Card Validator
followUps: What is the difference between meta name and meta property?
How do you prevent a page from being indexed?
What are rich snippets and how do you get them?
interviewTips: Know the essential meta tags from memory
Mention structured data/JSON-LD for bonus points
Show you understand that SEO is about content quality, not just meta tags

content.question: What are the different types of CSS selectors? Explain combinators.
content.answer:
CSS selectors target elements for styling:

1. Simple: element (div), class (.nav), ID (#header), universal (*)
2. Attribute: [type='text'], [href^='https'], [class~='active']
3. Combinators:
   - Descendant (space): div p (any p inside div)
   - Child (>): div > p (direct child p only)
   - Adjacent sibling (+): h2 + p (p immediately after h2)
   - General sibling (~): h2 ~ p (all p siblings after h2)
4. Pseudo-classes: :hover, :first-child, :nth-child()
5. Pseudo-elements: ::before, ::after, ::placeholder

codeExamples.title: Selector Types
codeExamples.explanation: Attribute selectors are powerful for targeting elements without adding classes. ^= starts with, $= ends with, *= contains.

codeExamples.title: Combinators
codeExamples.explanation: Child (>) is stricter than descendant (space). Adjacent (+) targets one element, general sibling (~) targets all matching siblings.

codeExamples.title: Practical Selector Patterns
codeExamples.explanation: Combining selectors with :not() and attribute selectors can replace many JavaScript-based style toggles.

notes: Prefer class selectors over element or ID selectors for maintainability
Avoid deeply nested selectors (more than 3 levels) for performance
:not() pseudo-class accepts simple selectors, not complex ones (in older CSS)
followUps: What is the difference between :nth-child and :nth-of-type?
How does the :not() pseudo-class work?
What are the performance implications of different selectors?
interviewTips: Know all four combinators and draw examples of what they match
Attribute selectors are rarely mentioned - showing knowledge of them impresses
Be able to write a selector for a specific element without changing the HTML

content.question: How does CSS specificity work? How do you calculate it?
content.answer:
Specificity determines which CSS rule wins when multiple rules target the same element.

Calculation (A, B, C):
- A: Number of ID selectors
- B: Number of class selectors, attribute selectors, pseudo-classes
- C: Number of element selectors, pseudo-elements

Cascade order (highest to lowest priority):
1. !important declarations
2. Inline styles (style attribute)
3. ID selectors (#id)
4. Class/attribute/pseudo-class selectors
5. Element/pseudo-element selectors
6. Universal selector (*) - zero specificity

Equal specificity: last rule in source order wins.
Inheritance: some properties inherit (color, font), others don't (margin, padding).

codeExamples.title: Specificity Calculation Examples
codeExamples.explanation: Compare specificity left to right: (1,0,0) always beats (0,99,99). IDs always beat classes, classes always beat elements.

codeExamples.title: Cascade and Inheritance
codeExamples.explanation: Inheritance flows from parent to child for text-related properties. Layout properties like margin and padding never inherit.

codeExamples.title: Avoiding Specificity Wars
codeExamples.explanation: Keep specificity low and flat. BEM naming (.block__element--modifier) helps avoid nesting. Only use !important for utilities or third-party overrides.

notes: Inline styles (1,0,0,0) beat any selector-based rule
!important trumps everything (even inline), but avoid it
The universal selector (*) has zero specificity
followUps: What is the difference between initial, inherit, and unset?
How do CSS layers (@layer) affect specificity?
How would you override a third-party stylesheet?
interviewTips: Be able to calculate specificity for any given selector on the spot
Know which properties inherit and which don't
Suggest BEM or CSS Modules as solutions to specificity issues

content.question: Explain the different CSS position values. How does z-index work?
content.answer:
CSS position values:

1. static (default): normal document flow, top/left/z-index ignored
2. relative: stays in flow, offset from its normal position
3. absolute: removed from flow, positioned relative to nearest positioned ancestor
4. fixed: removed from flow, positioned relative to viewport
5. sticky: hybrid - relative until scroll threshold, then fixed

Z-index only works on positioned elements (not static). It creates stacking contexts.

codeExamples.title: Position Values Compared
codeExamples.explanation: Absolute needs a positioned ancestor (relative is most common). Without one, it positions relative to the document body.

codeExamples.title: Z-Index and Stacking Context
codeExamples.explanation: Z-index is scoped to stacking contexts. A child can never appear above an element that is above its parent's stacking context.

codeExamples.title: Practical: Modal Overlay
codeExamples.explanation: Fixed positioning with inset: 0 creates a full-screen overlay. The modal inside uses relative positioning to be the anchor for the absolute close button.

notes: Sticky requires a top/bottom value to know when to 'stick'
Absolute elements are sized to their content unless given width/height
Use inset: 0 as shorthand for top: 0; right: 0; bottom: 0; left: 0
followUps: How does sticky positioning work inside a scrollable container?
What creates a new stacking context?
How do you center an absolute element?
interviewTips: Draw diagrams showing how each position value affects layout
Stacking context is a common gotcha - knowing it shows depth
Mention the inset shorthand to show modern CSS knowledge

content.question: How do CSS floats work? What is the clearfix hack?
content.answer:
Float was originally for text wrapping around images, later used for layouts (before Flexbox/Grid).

float: left/right removes the element from normal flow and pushes it to the left/right. Following content wraps around it.

Problems:
- Parent collapses (no height) when all children float
- Solution: clearfix hack or overflow: hidden on parent

clear: left/right/both prevents an element from wrapping next to floated elements.

Modern CSS: Use Flexbox/Grid instead. Floats are still useful for text wrapping around images.

codeExamples.title: Float Basics and Clearing
codeExamples.explanation: Floated elements are removed from normal flow. The parent doesn't know about them, so it collapses to zero height.

codeExamples.title: Clearfix Solutions
codeExamples.explanation: display: flow-root is the modern clearfix. It creates a Block Formatting Context (BFC) that contains floats without side effects like clipping overflow.

codeExamples.title: When Floats Are Still Useful
codeExamples.explanation: Float is still the best solution for text wrapping around images and drop caps. For everything else, use Flexbox or Grid.

notes: Floats are legacy for layouts - use Flexbox/Grid for modern layouts
display: flow-root is the cleanest clearfix solution
Block Formatting Context (BFC) contains floats and prevents margin collapse
followUps: What is a Block Formatting Context (BFC)?
Why did we stop using floats for layouts?
Can you achieve text wrapping with Flexbox?
interviewTips: Show you know floats are legacy but understand WHY they were used
Mention display: flow-root as the modern clearfix
Demonstrate you'd use Flexbox/Grid for layouts today

content.question: Explain Flexbox in depth. What are the container vs item properties?
content.answer:
Flexbox is a 1-dimensional layout system for arranging items in rows or columns.

Container properties (on parent):
- display: flex
- flex-direction: row | column | row-reverse | column-reverse
- justify-content: main axis alignment
- align-items: cross axis alignment
- flex-wrap: wrap | nowrap
- gap: spacing between items

Item properties (on children):
- flex-grow: how much to grow (0 = don't)
- flex-shrink: how much to shrink (1 = default)
- flex-basis: initial size before growing/shrinking
- flex: shorthand (grow shrink basis)
- align-self: override align-items for one item
- order: change visual order

codeExamples.title: Container Properties
codeExamples.explanation: justify-content controls the main axis (direction of flex-direction). align-items controls the cross axis (perpendicular to direction).

codeExamples.title: Item Properties and flex Shorthand
codeExamples.explanation: flex: 1 is the most common - items grow equally to fill space. flex: 0 0 <size> creates fixed-width items that don't flex.

codeExamples.title: Common Flexbox Patterns
codeExamples.explanation: These patterns cover 90% of Flexbox use cases. The sticky footer pattern with flex-direction: column and flex: 1 on main is extremely common.

notes: flex: 1 means flex: 1 1 0% (grow, shrink, zero basis)
gap works in Flexbox (not just Grid) in all modern browsers
Flex items cannot use float or clear
followUps: What is the difference between flex-basis and width?
How does flex-shrink work when items overflow?
When would you use order vs changing DOM order?
interviewTips: Know the difference between justify-content and align-items cold
Be able to code the centering, navbar, and sticky footer patterns
Understand the flex shorthand values

content.question: Explain CSS Grid in depth. When should you use Grid vs Flexbox?
content.answer:
CSS Grid is a 2-dimensional layout system controlling both rows and columns simultaneously.

Grid vs Flexbox:
- Grid: 2D (rows AND columns), good for page layouts and complex grids
- Flexbox: 1D (row OR column), good for component layouts and alignment

Key concepts:
- Explicit grid: defined with grid-template-columns/rows
- Implicit grid: auto-created for extra items
- fr unit: fraction of available space
- Grid lines: numbered lines between tracks
- Grid areas: named regions for placement

codeExamples.title: Grid Template and Placement
codeExamples.explanation: Grid lines are numbered starting at 1. Use -1 for the last line. grid-column: 1 / -1 spans the entire width.

codeExamples.title: Grid Template Areas
codeExamples.explanation: Grid template areas are the most readable way to define layouts. Each string is a row, each word is a column. Repeat a name to span cells.

codeExamples.title: Auto-fill Responsive Grid
codeExamples.explanation: repeat(auto-fill, minmax(280px, 1fr)) is the most powerful one-liner in CSS Grid - a fully responsive card grid with no media queries.

notes: auto-fill creates empty tracks, auto-fit collapses them - use auto-fill for consistent sizing
fr unit distributes free space AFTER fixed and min-content tracks are sized
Grid items can overlap - use z-index to control stacking
followUps: What is the difference between auto-fill and auto-fit?
How does the fr unit work?
What is CSS subgrid and when would you use it?
interviewTips: Know the auto-fill + minmax pattern by heart
Grid template areas are great for whiteboard interviews - very visual
Be clear about when to use Grid (2D) vs Flexbox (1D)

content.question: How do CSS transitions work? What properties can be transitioned?
content.answer:
CSS transitions animate property changes smoothly over a duration.

Syntax: transition: property duration timing-function delay

Key points:
- Only numeric/color properties can transition (width, color, opacity, transform)
- display and visibility cannot be smoothly transitioned
- Timing functions: ease, linear, ease-in, ease-out, ease-in-out, cubic-bezier()
- Performance: only transform and opacity are GPU-accelerated
- Put transition on the base state, not the :hover state

codeExamples.title: Transition Basics
codeExamples.explanation: Always put transition on the base state so it animates both on hover and off. Listing specific properties is better than 'all' for performance and predictability.

codeExamples.title: Timing Functions
codeExamples.explanation: ease is the default and works for most cases. Use cubic-bezier for custom physics. Chrome DevTools has a visual curve editor.

codeExamples.title: Practical Transition Patterns
codeExamples.explanation: Use opacity + visibility for show/hide (visibility: hidden is accessible, display: none is not). max-height trick works for expand/collapse.

notes: display: none cannot be transitioned - use opacity + visibility instead
Transitions on page load require JavaScript to add a class after load
Prefer transform and opacity for 60fps animations
followUps: What is the difference between transitions and animations?
How do you detect when a transition ends in JavaScript?
What properties should you avoid transitioning for performance?
interviewTips: Know that transform/opacity are GPU-accelerated (compositing only)
Mention the visibility trick for accessible show/hide
Be able to write a button hover effect from scratch

content.question: How do CSS animations differ from transitions? Explain @keyframes.
content.answer:
CSS animations use @keyframes for multi-step, auto-playing animations.

Differences from transitions:
- Transitions: triggered by state change (hover, class), 2 states only (A to B)
- Animations: can auto-play, loop, have multiple steps, run independently

Animation properties:
- animation-name: keyframe name
- animation-duration: how long
- animation-timing-function: easing
- animation-delay: wait before start
- animation-iteration-count: number or infinite
- animation-direction: normal, reverse, alternate
- animation-fill-mode: forwards (keep end state), backwards, both
- animation-play-state: running, paused

codeExamples.title: Keyframes and Animation Properties
codeExamples.explanation: from/to is shorthand for 0%/100%. Use percentages for multi-step animations. forwards keeps the final keyframe state after the animation ends.

codeExamples.title: Practical Animation Examples
codeExamples.explanation: The spinner and skeleton shimmer are used in every modern web app. Staggered delays create a cascading entrance effect.

codeExamples.title: Animation Performance and Accessibility
codeExamples.explanation: Always include prefers-reduced-motion for users with motion sensitivity. Only animate transform and opacity for smooth 60fps performance.

notes: animation-fill-mode: forwards is needed to keep the end state
Use prefers-reduced-motion to respect accessibility preferences
Multiple animations can be comma-separated on one element
followUps: How do you trigger an animation replay with JavaScript?
What is the Web Animations API?
How do animation-fill-mode values differ?
interviewTips: Know the spinner and skeleton shimmer patterns from memory
Always mention prefers-reduced-motion for accessibility
Understand when to use animations vs transitions

content.question: What CSS transform functions are available? How do 3D transforms work?
content.answer:
CSS transform applies 2D or 3D spatial modifications without affecting document flow.

2D functions: translate(), rotate(), scale(), skew()
3D functions: translate3d(), rotate3d(), perspective()

Key points:
- Transforms don't affect layout (other elements don't move)
- GPU-accelerated for smooth performance
- transform-origin changes the pivot point
- perspective() creates depth for 3D effects
- Transforms are composited (applied in order)
- Multiple transforms on one line are applied right-to-left

codeExamples.title: 2D Transform Functions
codeExamples.explanation: Transform order matters because each transform changes the coordinate system. translate then rotate gives different results than rotate then translate.

codeExamples.title: 3D Transforms and Perspective
codeExamples.explanation: perspective on the parent creates 3D space. transform-style: preserve-3d keeps children in 3D. backface-visibility: hidden hides the back of flipped elements.

codeExamples.title: Practical Transform Effects
codeExamples.explanation: The hover lift and image zoom are the most common transform patterns in modern web design. Overflow: hidden on the container clips the scaled image.

notes: Transforms are GPU-accelerated - prefer them over margin/top for animation
transform-origin defaults to center - change it for different pivot points
3D transforms need perspective on parent and preserve-3d for nested 3D
followUps: Why does transform order matter?
What is the difference between perspective() function and perspective property?
How does will-change affect transform performance?
interviewTips: Know the absolute centering trick with translate(-50%, -50%)
Be able to implement a card flip effect
Understand why transforms are preferred for animations (GPU compositing)

content.question: What is the difference between pseudo-classes and pseudo-elements?
content.answer:
Pseudo-classes select elements in a specific STATE (single colon :).
Pseudo-elements create virtual ELEMENTS (double colon ::).

Pseudo-classes:
- User action: :hover, :focus, :active, :focus-visible
- Structural: :first-child, :last-child, :nth-child(), :nth-of-type()
- State: :checked, :disabled, :valid, :invalid, :empty
- Negation: :not(), :is(), :where(), :has()

Pseudo-elements:
- ::before, ::after - insert content before/after
- ::first-line, ::first-letter - style text parts
- ::placeholder - style input placeholder
- ::selection - style highlighted text

codeExamples.title: Pseudo-Classes
codeExamples.explanation: :focus-visible is better than :focus for accessibility - it shows focus rings for keyboard users but not mouse users.

codeExamples.title: Pseudo-Elements
codeExamples.explanation: ::before and ::after require the content property (even if empty string). They're inline by default - set display: block or inline-block for sizing.

codeExamples.title: Modern Pseudo-Classes (:is, :where, :has)
codeExamples.explanation: :has() is the parent selector CSS never had. It styles a parent based on its children. :is() reduces repetition, :where() keeps zero specificity for easy overrides.

notes: Pseudo-elements use :: (double colon), pseudo-classes use : (single colon)
::before and ::after don't work on void elements (img, input, br)
:has() is supported in all modern browsers as of 2023
followUps: What is the difference between :nth-child and :nth-of-type?
How does :focus-visible differ from :focus?
What are some creative uses of ::before and ::after?
interviewTips: Know the double vs single colon convention
Mention :has() to show you know cutting-edge CSS
Be able to create a custom checkbox or tooltip with pseudo-elements

content.question: How do CSS custom properties work? How are they different from Sass variables?
content.answer:
CSS custom properties (variables) are declared with -- prefix and used with var() function.

Key differences from Sass variables:
1. CSS variables cascade and inherit through the DOM
2. CSS variables can be changed at runtime with JavaScript
3. CSS variables can be scoped to any selector (not just global)
4. CSS variables work with media queries dynamically
5. Sass variables are compiled away - they don't exist at runtime

CSS variables enable:
- Theming (dark mode, brand colors)
- Component-scoped values
- Runtime style changes without JavaScript
- Responsive design without duplicating properties

codeExamples.title: Declaration and Usage
codeExamples.explanation: Variables declared on :root are available everywhere. var() accepts a fallback value as second argument. Use calc() for computed values.

codeExamples.title: Theming with CSS Variables
codeExamples.explanation: Toggle the dark class on the html element, and every component updates instantly. No need to change individual element styles.

codeExamples.title: Component-Scoped Variables
codeExamples.explanation: Scoped variables make component variants clean - just override the variable instead of repeating all the properties.

notes: CSS variables are inherited by default (like color and font)
Use JavaScript element.style.setProperty('--name', 'value') to change at runtime
CSS variables cannot be used in media query conditions (only inside rules)
followUps: Can CSS variables be animated/transitioned?
How do you access CSS variables in JavaScript?
What are the limitations of CSS variables?
interviewTips: Know the runtime vs compile-time difference with Sass variables
Show a dark mode implementation with CSS variables
Demonstrate component variant pattern with scoped variables

content.question: What are the different CSS units? When should you use each?
content.answer:
CSS units fall into two categories: absolute and relative.

Absolute: px (pixels) - fixed, doesn't scale

Relative:
- rem: relative to root font-size (html element, default 16px)
- em: relative to parent's font-size
- %: relative to parent's dimension
- vw/vh: 1% of viewport width/height
- vmin/vmax: 1% of smaller/larger viewport dimension
- ch: width of '0' character
- dvh: dynamic viewport height (accounts for mobile browser chrome)

Best practices:
- rem for font-size (respects user preferences)
- rem or px for spacing/padding
- % or vw for widths
- vh or dvh for full-screen sections

codeExamples.title: rem vs em
codeExamples.explanation: rem is predictable because it always references the root. em compounds (each nested level multiplies), which can cause unexpected sizes.

codeExamples.title: Viewport Units
codeExamples.explanation: dvh solves the notorious mobile 100vh bug where content hides behind the browser chrome. clamp() is the modern way to do fluid typography.

codeExamples.title: When to Use Each Unit
codeExamples.explanation: rem for font sizes respects user zoom settings. px for things that shouldn't scale (borders, shadows). em in media queries has the best cross-browser support.

notes: Browser default font-size is 16px, so 1rem = 16px
Users can change their browser font-size - rem respects this, px doesn't
dvh/svh/lvh are newer units for mobile viewport - check browser support
followUps: Why use rem instead of px for font sizes?
What is the 62.5% font-size trick?
What is the difference between vw and %?
interviewTips: Know the difference between rem and em - most common unit question
Mention dvh for mobile - shows awareness of real-world mobile issues
clamp() for fluid typography is a strong modern CSS signal

content.question: How do CSS media queries work? What are common breakpoints?
content.answer:
Media queries apply styles conditionally based on device characteristics.

Syntax: @media (condition) { rules }

Media types: screen, print, all
Media features: width, height, orientation, prefers-color-scheme, prefers-reduced-motion, hover, pointer

Common breakpoints:
- 480px: small phones
- 640px: large phones
- 768px: tablets
- 1024px: laptops
- 1280px: desktops
- 1536px: large screens

Mobile-first uses min-width, desktop-first uses max-width.

codeExamples.title: Mobile-First Breakpoints
codeExamples.explanation: Mobile-first breakpoints build up complexity. Each larger breakpoint adds layout features. This is progressive enhancement.

codeExamples.title: Feature Queries and Preferences
codeExamples.explanation: Feature queries let you adapt to user preferences and device capabilities, not just screen size. hover: none detects touchscreens.

codeExamples.title: @supports Feature Detection
codeExamples.explanation: @supports is CSS feature detection (like Modernizr but native). Use it to provide fallbacks for newer CSS features.

notes: Use em in media queries for best cross-browser zoom behavior
prefers-color-scheme and prefers-reduced-motion are accessibility essentials
@supports lets you use progressive enhancement natively in CSS
followUps: What is the difference between @media and @supports?
How do you handle high-DPI (retina) displays?
What is the difference between hover: hover and hover: none?
interviewTips: Know common breakpoints and the mobile-first philosophy
Mention user preference media queries for accessibility bonus points
@supports shows you think about progressive enhancement

content.question: What is BEM methodology? Why is it useful for CSS architecture?
content.answer:
BEM stands for Block Element Modifier - a naming convention for CSS classes.

- Block: standalone component (.card, .navbar, .form)
- Element: part of a block, no meaning alone (.card__title, .card__image)
- Modifier: variation or state (.card--featured, .card__title--large)

Benefits:
1. Low specificity (all single class selectors)
2. Self-documenting (class name tells you the structure)
3. No naming conflicts (block scopes everything)
4. Easy to find in codebase (search for .card__)
5. No nesting needed (flat selectors)

Alternatives: SMACSS, OOCSS, Atomic CSS (Tailwind), CSS Modules, CSS-in-JS.

codeExamples.title: BEM Naming Convention
codeExamples.code:
Title

Description

Read more

Featured

Logo
  • Home
  • About
codeExamples.explanation: Block is the component name. Element uses __ (double underscore). Modifier uses -- (double dash). Never nest elements: card__body__title is wrong.

codeExamples.title: BEM CSS Rules
codeExamples.explanation: Every BEM selector has the same specificity (0,1,0) because they're all single classes. No specificity wars, no nesting, no !important needed.

codeExamples.title: BEM with Sass/SCSS
codeExamples.explanation: Sass & nesting generates flat BEM selectors. The source is organized hierarchically but the output is flat single-class selectors.

notes: Never nest BEM elements: .card__body__title is wrong. Use .card__title instead
Elements belong to blocks, not other elements
BEM works best with Sass/SCSS for developer experience
followUps: How does BEM compare to CSS Modules?
What are the drawbacks of BEM?
How do you handle deeply nested components in BEM?
interviewTips: Know BEM syntax: block, block__element, block--modifier
Explain WHY: low specificity, self-documenting, scalable
Compare BEM to CSS Modules or utility-first (Tailwind) approaches

content.question: What are CSS Modules and CSS-in-JS? What are the trade-offs?
content.answer:
Both solve CSS scoping (preventing style conflicts) in different ways.

CSS Modules: Write regular CSS, build tool generates unique class names.
- Scoped by default, no conflicts
- Zero runtime cost (compiled at build time)
- Regular CSS syntax, easy to learn
- Works with any framework

CSS-in-JS (styled-components, Emotion): Write CSS in JavaScript.
- Dynamic styles based on props/state
- Colocated with component code
- Runtime cost (generates CSS at runtime)
- Framework-specific (usually React)

Modern trend: Moving away from runtime CSS-in-JS toward zero-runtime solutions (CSS Modules, Tailwind, vanilla-extract, Panda CSS).

codeExamples.title: CSS Modules
codeExamples.explanation: CSS Modules add a unique hash to class names at build time. Zero runtime cost. Regular CSS syntax. Works with Vite, Webpack, etc.

codeExamples.title: CSS-in-JS (styled-components)
codeExamples.explanation: styled-components generates unique classes at runtime. Pros: dynamic styles with props, colocated code. Cons: runtime performance cost, larger bundle.

codeExamples.title: Comparison and Modern Alternatives
codeExamples.explanation: The industry is moving toward zero-runtime solutions. Tailwind is currently the most popular. CSS Modules remain excellent for traditional CSS workflows.

notes: Runtime CSS-in-JS (styled-components) adds ~12KB to bundle and has runtime cost
CSS Modules work out of the box with Vite and Next.js
Tailwind CSS has become the most popular styling approach in React ecosystem
followUps: What is the performance impact of runtime CSS-in-JS?
How does Tailwind CSS compare to CSS Modules?
What is vanilla-extract?
interviewTips: Know the trade-offs of each approach - there's no single right answer
Mention the industry trend away from runtime CSS-in-JS
Pick one approach and explain why you'd choose it for a given project

content.question: What is Sass/SCSS? What features does it add to CSS?
content.answer:
Sass (Syntactically Awesome Style Sheets) is a CSS preprocessor that compiles to regular CSS.

SCSS (Sassy CSS) uses CSS-like syntax with braces. Sass uses indentation-based syntax.

Key features:
1. Variables ($color-primary: blue)
2. Nesting (reduces repetition)
3. Mixins (reusable style blocks with parameters)
4. Partials & imports (split CSS into files)
5. Functions (compute values)
6. Extend/inheritance (@extend .base-class)
7. Operators (math in CSS)
8. Control flow (@if, @for, @each)

Note: Modern CSS now has variables, nesting, and :is(). Sass is less essential but still widely used.

codeExamples.title: Variables, Nesting, and Partials
codeExamples.explanation: Partials (prefixed with _) don't generate separate CSS files. @use replaces @import (which is deprecated). & refers to the parent selector.

codeExamples.title: Mixins and Functions
codeExamples.explanation: Mixins insert blocks of CSS (@include). Functions return values. Mixins with @content act as wrappers - perfect for media queries.

codeExamples.title: Extend, Loops, and Maps
codeExamples.explanation: % placeholder selectors only output when extended. @each loops generate repetitive classes (like Tailwind's utility classes). String interpolation uses #{$var}.

notes: Use @use instead of @import (deprecated in modern Sass)
Don't over-nest - max 3 levels deep is a good rule
Modern CSS now has nesting and variables - evaluate if you still need Sass
followUps: What is the difference between @use and @import in Sass?
When would you choose Sass over plain CSS in a new project?
What is the difference between a mixin and @extend?
interviewTips: Know mixins, variables, and nesting at minimum
Mention that modern CSS is closing the gap with native nesting and variables
Show you understand build tools compile Sass to CSS

content.question: Explain the browser rendering pipeline. What are reflow and repaint?
content.answer:
The browser rendering pipeline converts HTML/CSS/JS into pixels on screen:

1. Parse HTML -> DOM tree
2. Parse CSS -> CSSOM tree
3. Combine -> Render tree (visible elements only)
4. Layout (Reflow): calculate position and size of every element
5. Paint: fill in pixels (colors, borders, shadows, text)
6. Composite: combine layers and draw to screen

Reflow (Layout): triggered when geometry changes (width, height, position, font-size). Most expensive.
Repaint: triggered when appearance changes without geometry (color, background, visibility).
Composite-only: transform, opacity - cheapest, GPU-accelerated.

Performance tip: animate only transform and opacity for 60fps.

codeExamples.title: What Triggers Reflow, Repaint, Composite
codeExamples.explanation: Every CSS property falls into one of these categories. The rendering pipeline runs in order: a reflow triggers repaint + composite. A repaint triggers composite. Composite-only skips everything.

codeExamples.title: Optimizing Rendering Performance
codeExamples.explanation: will-change promotes an element to its own GPU layer. contain limits the scope of recalculations. content-visibility skips rendering offscreen content entirely.

codeExamples.title: Batching DOM Reads and Writes
codeExamples.explanation: Layout thrashing happens when JS interleaves DOM reads and writes. Each read forces a synchronous layout. Batch reads first, writes second.

notes: Chrome DevTools Performance tab shows reflows, repaints, and composite layers
Forced synchronous layout is the most common performance issue in JS-heavy pages
contain: content is a safe default for isolated components
followUps: What is the Critical Rendering Path?
How does CSS block rendering?
What is layout thrashing and how do you avoid it?
interviewTips: Knowing the rendering pipeline shows deep frontend understanding
Explain reflow vs repaint vs composite with concrete CSS properties
Mention Chrome DevTools Performance tab for profiling

content.question: What are Core Web Vitals? How does CSS affect them?
content.answer:
Core Web Vitals are Google's key metrics for user experience (affect SEO ranking):

1. LCP (Largest Contentful Paint): time for largest visible element to render. Target: < 2.5s
2. CLS (Cumulative Layout Shift): visual stability (elements jumping around). Target: < 0.1
3. INP (Interaction to Next Paint, replaced FID): responsiveness to user input. Target: < 200ms

CSS impact on Web Vitals:
- LCP: render-blocking CSS, large images, custom fonts
- CLS: images without dimensions, dynamically injected content, web fonts causing FOIT/FOUT
- INP: long-running CSS animations, expensive selectors

codeExamples.title: Preventing CLS (Layout Shifts)
codeExamples.explanation: CLS is caused by elements that change size or position after initial render. Always reserve space for images, ads, and dynamic content.

codeExamples.title: Improving LCP
codeExamples.explanation: LCP measures when the largest visible element renders. Preload critical resources, inline critical CSS, and never lazy-load above-the-fold images.

codeExamples.title: CSS Performance Best Practices
codeExamples.explanation: content-visibility: auto can dramatically improve initial page load by skipping rendering of offscreen content. contain: content isolates recalculation scope.

notes: Core Web Vitals are a Google ranking factor since 2021
Measure with Lighthouse, PageSpeed Insights, or Chrome UX Report
FID was replaced by INP (Interaction to Next Paint) in March 2024
followUps: What is the Critical Rendering Path?
How do you measure Core Web Vitals in the field?
What is the difference between FCP, LCP, and TTFB?
interviewTips: Know all three Core Web Vitals and their target values
Be able to explain CSS-specific optimizations for each metric
Mention content-visibility and contain for advanced optimization knowledge

content.question: How do you implement responsive images? What are modern image formats?
content.answer:
Responsive images serve the right image size for the user's device/viewport.

Techniques:
1. srcset + sizes: browser picks the best resolution
2. picture element: art direction (different crops/formats)
3. CSS object-fit: control how images fill containers
4. loading='lazy': defer offscreen images
5. fetchpriority='high': prioritize above-fold images

Modern formats:
- WebP: 25-35% smaller than JPEG, supports transparency
- AVIF: 50% smaller than JPEG, excellent quality
- SVG: vector graphics, infinite scaling
- Use picture element for format fallbacks.

codeExamples.title: Responsive Images with srcset
codeExamples.explanation: The browser uses srcset + sizes to automatically pick the optimal image file. This saves bandwidth on mobile and looks sharp on retina displays.

codeExamples.title: Picture Element for Art Direction and Formats
codeExamples.explanation: picture element gives full control: serve AVIF to modern browsers, WebP as fallback, JPEG for legacy. Browser picks the first supported source.

codeExamples.title: CSS Object-Fit and Image Optimization
codeExamples.explanation: object-fit: cover is the most common - it fills the container while maintaining aspect ratio, cropping edges if needed. Like background-size: cover for img elements.

notes: Always include width and height attributes on images to prevent CLS
Use loading='lazy' for below-the-fold images, loading='eager' for hero images
AVIF is 50% smaller than JPEG but has slower encoding - great for static sites
followUps: What is the difference between object-fit: cover and contain?
When would you use SVG vs PNG?
How do you optimize images for web performance?
interviewTips: Know srcset + sizes syntax - it comes up in performance interviews
Mention AVIF/WebP with picture fallback for modern optimization
Always mention CLS prevention (width/height/aspect-ratio)

content.question: What are CSS container queries? How do they differ from media queries?
content.answer:
Container queries style elements based on their PARENT container's size, not the viewport.

Media queries: respond to viewport width (global)
Container queries: respond to container width (component-level)

This enables truly reusable responsive components that adapt to wherever they're placed - sidebar, main content, or full-width.

Setup:
1. Define a container: container-type: inline-size on parent
2. Query it: @container (min-width: 400px) { }

Container query units: cqw, cqh, cqi, cqb (like vw/vh but for container).

codeExamples.title: Basic Container Query
codeExamples.explanation: The card layout changes based on its container width, not the viewport. The same card component works in a narrow sidebar and a wide main area.

codeExamples.title: Container Query Units
codeExamples.explanation: Container query units are like viewport units (vw, vh) but relative to the container. They enable fluid sizing that adapts to the component's context.

codeExamples.title: Real-World: Adaptive Component
codeExamples.explanation: This product card adapts its layout based on how much space it has, regardless of viewport size. Drop it in any container and it just works.

notes: container-type: inline-size is the most common (query width only)
Container queries have full browser support since Feb 2023
Container queries enable truly portable, context-aware components
followUps: Can you nest container queries?
What is the performance impact of container queries?
How do container queries work with CSS Grid?
interviewTips: Container queries are the biggest CSS advancement for component architecture
Compare them to media queries to show you understand the paradigm shift
Mention they're fully supported in all modern browsers

content.question: What are the newest CSS features? Explain :has(), native nesting, and @layer.
content.answer:
Modern CSS (2023-2025) introduced game-changing features:

1. :has() - parent selector: style parent based on children
2. Native CSS Nesting - nest selectors like Sass (no preprocessor needed)
3. @layer - cascade layers for controlling specificity order
4. @scope - scope styles to a DOM subtree
5. Subgrid - child grids inheriting parent grid tracks

These features reduce the need for preprocessors (Sass) and CSS-in-JS, making vanilla CSS much more powerful.

codeExamples.title: :has() - The Parent Selector
codeExamples.explanation: :has() is the most requested CSS feature ever. It lets you select parents and previous siblings, which was previously impossible without JavaScript.

codeExamples.title: Native CSS Nesting
codeExamples.explanation: Native CSS nesting works like Sass but in the browser with no build step. The & symbol references the parent selector. Supported in all modern browsers since 2023.

codeExamples.title: @layer - Cascade Layers
codeExamples.explanation: @layer controls the cascade independently from specificity. Later layers override earlier ones regardless of selector specificity. This solves specificity wars.

notes: :has() is supported in all browsers since Dec 2023
Native nesting makes Sass less necessary for most projects
@layer is especially useful for managing third-party CSS conflicts
followUps: How does @layer interact with !important?
What is @scope and how does it differ from CSS Modules?
Can you use :has() for previous-sibling selection?
interviewTips: Knowing these features shows you stay current with CSS evolution
:has() is the most impressive modern CSS feature to demonstrate
Mention that native CSS now replaces many Sass features

content.question: How do you implement print styles and dark mode in CSS?
content.answer:
Print styles optimize pages for printing. Dark mode adapts to user's OS theme preference.

Print styles:
- Use @media print { } to add print-specific rules
- Hide navigation, footers, ads
- Use black text on white background
- Set page margins with @page
- Force background printing with print-color-adjust

Dark mode:
- prefers-color-scheme: dark media query (OS preference)
- Class-based toggle (.dark) for manual control
- CSS custom properties for theme values
- color-scheme meta tag for native form styling

codeExamples.title: Print Styles
codeExamples.explanation: Print styles remove UI elements, optimize for paper, and show link URLs. break-inside: avoid prevents awkward page breaks in the middle of elements.

codeExamples.title: Dark Mode with CSS Variables
codeExamples.explanation: CSS variables make dark mode trivial - change the variable values and every component updates. color-scheme tells the browser to style checkboxes, scrollbars, etc.

codeExamples.title: Dark Mode JavaScript Toggle
codeExamples.code:
<!-- Meta tag for native control styling -->
<meta name="color-scheme" content="light dark" />

<script>
// Check stored preference, then OS preference
function getTheme() {
  const stored = localStorage.getItem('theme');
  if (stored) return stored;
  return window.matchMedia(
    '(prefers-color-scheme: dark)'
  ).matches ? 'dark' : 'light';
}

// Apply theme
function setTheme(theme) {
  document.documentElement.classList
    .toggle('dark', theme === 'dark');
  localStorage.setItem('theme', theme);
}

// Initialize
setTheme(getTheme());

// Toggle button
document.getElementById('themeToggle')
  .addEventListener('click', () => {
    const current = document.documentElement
      .classList.contains('dark') ? 'light' : 'dark';
    setTheme(current);
  });

// Listen for OS changes
window.matchMedia('(prefers-color-scheme: dark)')
  .addEventListener('change', (e) => {
    if (!localStorage.getItem('theme')) {
      setTheme(e.matches ? 'dark' : 'light');
    }
  });
</script>

<style>
/* Smooth theme transition */
html {
  transition: background 0.3s ease, color 0.3s ease;
}
</style>
codeExamples.explanation: The three-tier approach: 1) check localStorage for user preference, 2) fall back to OS preference, 3) default to light. Listen for OS changes too.

notes: Always test print styles with browser Print Preview (Ctrl/Cmd + P)
color-scheme property styles native form controls for dark mode
Prevent flash of wrong theme by setting class before page renders
followUps: How do you prevent a flash of unstyled content (FOUC) with dark mode?
What is the difference between color-scheme and prefers-color-scheme?

interviewTips: Print styles are rarely asked but impressive to know
Dark mode is very commonly asked - show the CSS variables approach
Mention the three-tier preference system (stored > OS > default)
